mnemonics.title: 5 Actors in NIST Cloud
mnemonics.content: CPCBE - Cloud Providers Consumers Brokering Auditing Everyone

mnemonics.title: Service Models
mnemonics.content: SIP - Software is PaaS, IaaS provides Pizza as a Service (build your own)

mnemonics.title: Deployment Models
mnemonics.content: PCH (Punjab Chandigarh Haryana) - Public, Community, Hybrid (wait Private should be there) -修正: P-PCH: Public, Private, Community, Hybrid

mnemonics.title: Essential Characteristics
mnemonics.content: ORERM - On-demand, Resource pooling, Elasticity, Measured service, Broad network access

mnemonics.title: Case Study Analysis Steps
mnemonics.content: A-SEC-CAP: Analyze requirements → Select deployment → Evaluate security → Choose service model → Check compliance → Assess performance

acronyms.expansion: National Institute of Standards and Technology
acronyms.usage: US federal agency that defines cloud computing standards and reference architecture
NIST's conceptual framework showing actors, activities, and interactions in cloud computing
Virtualized computing resources (servers, storage, networks) - like renting hardware
Development/deployment platforms - like renting a workspace with tools
Ready-to-use applications - like subscribing to ready software
Contract defining expected service quality, uptime, and responsibilities
Organization providing cloud services (AWS, Azure, GCP, etc.)
Intermediary managing multiple cloud services for consumers
Virtual desktop infrastructure delivered as a cloud service

visualTips.visualization: Draw a triangle with Cloud Provider at top, Cloud Consumer at bottom-left, Cloud Broker in middle, Cloud Carrier as bridge, Cloud Auditor as watchdog on side - shows flow of services and monitoring
Stack diagram: IaaS at bottom (foundation), PaaS in middle, SaaS on top - like a pyramid where each builds on previous, showing increasing abstraction and decreasing control
Imagine a large apartment building (data center) with many tenants (users) sharing common resources (water, electricity, elevator) but each having private space
Picture a rubber band that stretches (scales up) and shrinks (scales down) based on demand - shows dynamic resource allocation
For any case study, draw: User Layer → Front-end → Back-end Cloud Services → Data Storage → Management Plane → Security Layer - label each component with NIST terminology

analogies.analogy: Like moving from owning a house (traditional IT - you maintain everything) to staying in a hotel (cloud - you pay for what you use, services included)
Similar to a shared office workspace - multiple companies share meeting rooms, printers, internet, but each has private offices/desks
Like an ATM - you get cash (resources) instantly without bank teller (human intervention) when you need it
Like a travel agent who compares airlines and hotels (cloud providers) to find best deal for travelers (consumers)
Like electricity billing - you pay for exact amount consumed, meter tracks usage, billed accordingly
Like apartments in a building - tenants share structure but have locked doors, separate walls, and cannot access each other's spaces
Like using both personal wallet (private data/traditional IT) and credit card (public cloud for overflow) for different expenses
Like a blueprint for building a house - defines standard components, relationships, and best practices regardless of who builds it
